quoteFirst: It was not known whether or no the nailer had seen the apparition; but it was pretty certain he believed in it; and as the "Dropping Cave" is both dark and solitary, and had forty years ago a bad name to boot--for the mermaid had been observed disporting in front of it even
quoteLast: , and lights and screams heard from it at nights--it must have been a rather formidable place to a man living in the momentary expectation of a visit from a dead wife. So far as could be ascertained--for the nailer himself was rather close in the matter--he had not entered the cave at all. He seemed, judging from the marks of scraping left along the sides for about two or three feet from the narrow opening, to have taken his stand outside, where the light was good, and the way of retreat clear, and to have raked outwards to him, as far as he could reach, all that stuck to
title: My Schools and Schoolmasters; Or, The Story of My Education
author: Miller, Hugh, 1802-1856

quoteFirst: The Highlanders, when they entered the town in the morning, had secured the Heralds and Pursuivants:
quoteLast: they surrounded the Cross with a body of armed men, and obliged the Heralds to proclaim King James, to read the Commission of Regency, and the Declaration, dated at Rome, in December 1743, with a Manifesto in the name of Charles Prince Regent, dated at Paris, 16th of May, 1745.
title:
The Jacobite Rebellions (1689-1746)
(Bell's Scottish History Source Books.)
author: Thomson, J. Pringle (James Pringle), 1889-1916

quoteFirst: Warren's Hamburg contract, and so I up and to W. Coventry's (he and G. Carteret being the party concerned in it), and after conference with him about it to satisfaction I home again to the office.
quoteLast: home to dinner, and then all the afternoon busy to prepare an answer to this demand of the Commissioners of Accounts, and did discourse with Sir W. Warren about it, and so in the evening with my wife and Deb.
title: Diary of Samuel Pepys — Volume 66: June/July 1668
author: Pepys, Samuel, 1633-1703; Braybrooke, Richard Griffin, Baron, 1783-1858 [Editor]; Wheatley, Henry B. (Henry Benjamin), 1838-1917 [Editor]; Bright, Mynors, 1818-1883 [Translator]

quoteLast: on a grim December day, the 13th of that stern month, the _Thurifer_ was seized by the tug and dragged out into the river, looking most ungainly and helpless among the huge trim liners lying easily at anchor on the bosom of the grey Mersey as she submitted clumsily to the fussy efforts of the great Jolliffe tug.
title: Frank Brown, Sea Apprentice
author: Bullen, Frank Thomas, 1857-1915; Twidle, Arthur, 1865-1936 [Illustrator]

quoteFirst: First I had an idea it meant a coming storm, but there wasn't a sign of a cloud in sight. It seemed to be a deep, heavy reverberation, just like I've heard dynamite make at the red-sandstone quarry near Chester when the workmen
quoteLast: set off their blasts. Of course you noticed it, too, Jack?"
title: Jack Winters' Campmates
author: Overton, Mark

quoteFirst: They got back to their rooms
quoteLast: , to find that William Perry was up and dressed, and impatient to go out.
title: Ned Wilding's Disappearance; or, The Darewell Chums in the City
author: Chapman, Allen

quoteLast: . It is now ten minutes past eleven. Are you curious, Willoughby? I confess to curiosity. I am an inquisitive methodical person, and when a man gets a telegram bidding him tell Trench something and he tells Trench nothing, I am curious as a philosopher to know what that something is! Castleton is the only other officer of our regiment in London.
title: The Four Feathers
author: Mason, A. E. W. (Alfred Edward Woodley), 1865-1948

quoteFirst: He is known to the official department, of which the magistrates are a constituent part, as a notorious ----l; and his better-half, who, by-the-way, is what is called a free-trader, meaning, to save the rascality of a husband, sells liquor by small portions, to suit the Murphys and the O'Neals. But, as it pleases our Mr. Dunn, he very often becomes a more than profitable customer, and may be found snoring out the penalty in some sequestered place, too frequently for his own character. Between the hours of ten and
quoteLast: , Dunn, if not too much incapacitated, may be seen limping his way down Broad street, to watch vessels arriving and departing, carrying a limp-cane in one hand, and a large covered whip in the other. We were struck with the appearance of the latter, because it was similar to those carried in the hands of a rough, menial class of men in Macon, Georgia, who called themselves marshals, under a misapplication of the term.
title: Manuel Pereira; Or, The Sovereign Rule of South Carolina
author: Adams, F. Colburn (Francis Colburn), 1850-1891

quoteLast: . At the same time the bugles of the Prussian soldiers sounded under our windows, where they had come to drill.
title:
The Childrens' Story of the War, Volume 1 (of 10)
From the Beginning of the War to the Landing of the British Army in France
author: Parrott, Edward, 1863-1921

quoteFirst: 20th. Up, with much pain in my eare and palate. To the office out of humour all the morning.
quoteLast: dined, and with my wife to the King's house, but there found the bill torn down and no play acted, and so being in the humour to see one, went to the Duke of York's house, and there saw "The Witts" again, which likes me better than it did the other day, having much wit in it. Here met with Mr.
title: Diary of Samuel Pepys — Complete 1667 N.S.
author: Pepys, Samuel, 1633-1703; Braybrooke, Richard Griffin, Baron, 1783-1858 [Editor]; Wheatley, Henry B. (Henry Benjamin), 1838-1917 [Editor]; Bright, Mynors, 1818-1883 [Translator]
